topic: Soils of India — Types, Distribution & Characteristics

questions.text: Which type of soil is most widely distributed in India?
questions.explanation: Alluvial soil is the most widely distributed soil in India, covering approximately 43% of the country's total land area. It covers the vast Indo-Gangetic Plain (from Punjab to Assam), river valleys, and coastal delta regions. Alluvial soil is also the most fertile and productive — supporting the majority of India's food grain production. Red soil covers about 18% (second largest) and black soil about 15%. Don't confuse 'most widespread' with 'most important for cotton' (that's black/Regur soil).

questions.text: Black soil, also called Regur, is best suited for growing which crop?
questions.explanation: Black soil (Regur) is best suited for cotton cultivation — hence it is also called 'cotton soil'. This is because black soil has exceptional moisture retention (holds water for months after rains end), which cotton requires for its long growing season. Black soil is most extensively found in the Deccan Plateau (Maharashtra, Gujarat, MP) — the same region that became India's cotton textile heartland. The term 'Regur' comes from Telugu. Rice grows in alluvial/peaty soil; tea grows in laterite/mountain soil; jute grows in alluvial/delta soil.

questions.text: The red colour of red soil is due to:
options: High content of humus and organic matter
Presence of iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) — ferric oxide
Basaltic lava that contains red minerals
questions.explanation: The red colour of red soil is caused by iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) — ferric oxide — which forms when iron-bearing minerals in the parent rock (ancient crystalline and metamorphic rocks) are oxidised in well-drained conditions. The same iron in its hydrated form (Fe₂O₃·H₂O) is yellow-ochre coloured — which is why red soil turns yellow in waterlogged valleys where iron is in the hydrated state. Red soil is found extensively in peninsular India (Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, AP, Odisha, Chhattisgarh).

questions.text: Which soil is formed in tropical areas with heavy rainfall and alternating dry periods, characterized by the removal of silica and bases through leaching?
questions.explanation: Laterite soil forms in tropical regions with alternating wet and dry seasons. Heavy monsoon rains leach out silica, lime, and other bases from the soil, leaving behind a residue enriched in iron and aluminium hydroxides, which harden into a crust on exposure to air. The name comes from the Latin 'later' (brick) because it can be cut into blocks used as building material. Laterite is infertile (leached of nutrients), acidic, found on hilltops, and supports plantation crops like tea, coffee, and rubber.

questions.text: What is the unique property of black soil (Regur) that no other major soil type in India possesses?
options: High iron content that makes it black
Self-ploughing (self-mulching) — it develops cracks in dry season and swells to seal them when wet
It is the only soil type that can grow both cotton and wheat
It is derived from alluvial deposits in the Deccan
questions.explanation: The unique feature of black soil is self-ploughing (also called self-mulching). During dry periods, the soil develops deep vertical cracks (due to shrinkage of clay minerals). Organic matter and fine particles from the surface fall into these cracks. When rain comes, the soil absorbs water, swells, and closes the cracks — effectively stirring itself. This keeps the soil aerated and mixed without ploughing. This property is caused by the high content of montmorillonite clay minerals, which expand and contract dramatically with water changes. No other major Indian soil type does this.

questions.text: Why does laterite soil form on hilltops rather than in valleys, even though it forms due to heavy rainfall?
options: Hilltops receive more direct rainfall than valleys
Hilltops have better drainage — intense leaching removes silica and bases, leaving iron/aluminium oxides behind. Valleys have gentler drainage and accumulate alluvium instead
The parent rock on hilltops is more susceptible to weathering than valley rocks
Hilltops are closer to the sun and experience more evaporation, concentrating minerals
questions.explanation: Laterite formation requires two conditions: (1) heavy rainfall to leach minerals, and (2) efficient drainage so the leached water moves away quickly and doesn't re-deposit minerals. Hilltops have both — rain falls, leaches silica and bases downward, and water drains away leaving iron/aluminium oxides behind. In valleys, water collects and drains slowly, so leaching is less intense. Instead, valleys accumulate alluvial or red soil washed from the hills. This is why you see laterite on the high Western Ghats hilltops and red/alluvial soil in the valleys below.

questions.text: What is the difference between Khadar and Bangar in alluvial soils?
options: Khadar is old alluvium with kankars; Bangar is new alluvium near rivers
Khadar is new alluvium near river channels (more fertile); Bangar is old alluvium away from channels (contains kankars)
Khadar is found only in Punjab; Bangar is found only in the Ganga delta
Khadar has more clay; Bangar has more sand
questions.explanation: Khadar is the newer alluvium deposited by rivers close to their current channels — lighter in colour, sandier, regularly renewed by floods, and slightly more fertile. Bangar is older alluvium deposited on elevated terraces away from river channels — darker, heavier in texture, and contains kankars (calcareous concretions — nodules of calcium carbonate/CaCO₃) that have accumulated over thousands of years. Khadar > Bangar in fertility. Both are types of alluvial soil but represent different ages of deposition. This distinction is frequently tested in SSC CGL and CHSL.

questions.text: Peaty soil has the highest organic content of all Indian soils but is agriculturally limited. Why?
options: Peaty soil is too sandy and loses water quickly
Peaty soil is waterlogged, highly acidic (pH 3.5-5.5), and deficient in phosphate and potash despite being rich in organic matter
Peaty soil is too alkaline and contains excess calcium
Peaty soil only forms in the Himalayas and is too cold for cultivation
questions.explanation: Peaty soil forms in permanently or seasonally waterlogged conditions where organic matter accumulates faster than it decomposes (because anaerobic/oxygen-free conditions prevent breakdown). While this creates high organic/humus content (40-50%), the same waterlogged conditions make it highly acidic (pH 3.5-5.5) and deficient in phosphate and potash. The soil is also difficult to drain and aerate. It mainly supports wetland rice (paddy) cultivation. Kuttanad in Kerala (below sea-level rice fields) and Bengal's delta areas are classic examples.

questions.text: Alkaline/saline soils (Usar/Reh) in India are reclaimed using which chemical treatment?
options: Gypsum (calcium sulphate) — which replaces excess sodium ions with calcium, reducing alkalinity
Urea — to add nitrogen and break down salt crusts
Potassium nitrate — to neutralise the sodium salts
questions.explanation: Alkaline (sodic) soils like Usar/Reh have excess sodium ions (Na⁺) that damage soil structure and prevent plant growth. The primary chemical reclamation method is gypsum (CaSO₄ — calcium sulphate) treatment: gypsum dissolves, releasing Ca²⁺ which replaces Na⁺ on the soil's clay particles. The displaced sodium washes away through proper drainage, reducing alkalinity. Gypsum is effective, cheap, and widely used in Punjab, Haryana, and UP reclamation projects. Liming would INCREASE pH (already too high); urea is a nitrogen fertiliser, not a reclamation agent.

questions.text: The word 'laterite' has an interesting origin. What does it mean and why was that name chosen?
options: From Latin 'latus' (wide) — because laterite soils cover wide plateau regions
From Latin 'later' (brick) — because moist laterite can be cut into blocks that harden on exposure to air and make excellent building material
From Greek 'lithos' (stone) — because it is the hardest of all Indian soils
From Arabic 'latir' (red) — because of its characteristic red-brick colour
questions.explanation: The name 'laterite' comes from the Latin word 'later' meaning brick. The name was coined because moist laterite soil can be cut into uniform blocks using simple tools — and these blocks harden on exposure to air and sunlight, creating durable building material. Ancient temples in Kerala (like those at Thrissur and Malappuram) and Karnataka were constructed using laterite blocks. This hardening property is unique to laterite among soil types. The high iron and aluminium oxide content causes the hardening reaction.

questions.text: Which farming technique involves ploughing along contour lines (horizontal lines of equal elevation on a slope) to reduce soil erosion?
questions.explanation: Contour ploughing (contour farming) involves ploughing furrows along the contour lines of a slope — that is, horizontally — rather than up and down the slope. When furrows run horizontally, they act as small barriers that catch and hold rainwater, preventing it from running downhill and carrying topsoil with it. This is especially important on gently sloping agricultural land in the peninsular plateau and lower Himalayan foothills. Terrace farming cuts the slope into steps (more extreme modification for steeper slopes). Strip cropping alternates crop strips. Shelterbelts are windbreaks (rows of trees).

questions.text: The Chambal valley in Rajasthan/MP is notorious for which type of soil erosion that has created its famous ravines (badlands)?
questions.explanation: The Chambal valley in Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh is the classic example of gully erosion in India. Running water cuts deep, narrow channels (gullies) into the soft alluvial/red soil, which progressively widen and deepen into a labyrinthine badlands landscape of ravines and ravines. These ravines (called 'beehad' in Hindi) historically provided cover for dacoits (Chambal ravines are famous in popular culture). Gully erosion is particularly severe in the Yamuna-Chambal basin and along the Mahanadi ravines in MP. Wind erosion is the dominant type in Rajasthan's Thar Desert.

questions.text:
Match the soil type with its most characteristic crop:
1. Black (Regur) soil — A. Tea
2. Alluvial soil — B. Cotton
3. Laterite soil — C. Wheat/Rice (all crops)
4. Red soil — D. Groundnut/Millets
questions.explanation: Black (Regur) soil → Cotton (B) — moisture retention makes it ideal for cotton. Alluvial soil → wheat, rice, all crops (C) — most fertile, grows everything. Laterite soil → Tea, coffee, rubber (A) — acidic, leached, best for plantation crops. Red soil → Groundnut, millets (D) — porous and low-nutrient; drought-resistant crops like groundnut and millets suit it. This matching pattern (soil-to-crop) is one of the most frequently repeated SSC question types in Geography.

questions.text: Which of the following regions of India is correctly matched with its predominant soil type?
options: Indo-Gangetic Plain (Punjab to Assam) — Black soil
questions.explanation: The Deccan Plateau — especially in Maharashtra, Gujarat, and parts of MP — is covered by black (Regur) soil, formed from the weathering of the ancient Deccan Trap basalt (65 million year old lava flows). The Indo-Gangetic Plain has alluvial soil (deposited by Himalayan rivers). Kerala's coastal hills have laterite soil (formed by heavy tropical rainfall leaching). Red soil dominates Tamil Nadu, Karnataka uplands, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, and Jharkhand.

questions.text: Which soil type has the HIGHEST humus / organic matter content among all Indian soils?
questions.explanation: Peaty soil (marshy soil) has the highest organic matter / humus content of all Indian soil types — it can be 40–50% organic matter by weight. This is because peaty soils form in permanently waterlogged, anaerobic (oxygen-free) conditions where organic matter accumulates faster than bacteria can decompose it. Despite this high organic content, peaty soil is acidic, waterlogged, and deficient in minerals — limiting its use mainly to wetland rice cultivation. Black soil also has good humus content but nowhere near as high as peaty soil.

questions.text: The 'self-ploughing' property of black soil is caused by which mineral characteristic?
options: High calcium carbonate content that crystallises and cracks the soil when dry
Presence of montmorillonite clay that expands when wet and shrinks/cracks when dry
Iron oxide crystals that expand on oxidation and contract on reduction
High potassium content that draws water into the soil and releases it cyclically
questions.explanation: The self-ploughing (self-mulching) property of black soil is due to its high content of montmorillonite clay minerals (also called smectite). Montmorillonite has a unique layer structure that absorbs large amounts of water and expands dramatically (up to 3x its dry volume), then shrinks and cracks deeply when water is removed. These alternating expansions and contractions stir the soil naturally. The high montmorillonite content comes from the parent basaltic rock. This property is unique to black/Regur soil in India and is why it can sustain cotton crops with minimal tillage compared to other soils.
